Summary: This study investigates the utility of static and dynamic 18F-DOPA PET radiomics in differentiating high-grade glioma (HGG) progression from treatment-related changes (TRC). The study compares the diagnostic performance of radiomics-based machine learning models to the current PET imaging standard of care, finding that radiomics provides only marginal additional value over classical analysis based on TBR values.

Item1.reason: The study mentions adherence to IBSI guidelines for radiomic feature extraction.

Item2.reason: The study explicitly defines inclusion and exclusion criteria for patient selection, aiming for a representative study population.

Item3.reason: The reference standard is based on histopathology or clinical-radiology follow-up based on RANO criteria, which is a well-established standard.

Item4.reason: The study uses data from two different institutions (CHRU of Nancy and Pitié-Salpêtrière hospital in Paris, France).

Item5.reason: The imaging data source is 18F-DOPA PET, which is a relevant imaging technique in neuro-oncology, and the study compares its performance to the current clinical standard of PET imaging care.

Item6.reason: The study reports the image acquisition parameters for the different PET systems used, including details on reconstruction algorithms and voxel sizes.

Item7.reason: The study includes only patients with a minimum 3-month interval between the end of radiation therapy and the 18F-FDOPA PET acquisition to reduce the risk of false positives. The final diagnoses were determined from histopathology or clinical-radiology follow-up during a 6-month follow-up period.

Condition1.reason: The study includes segmentation of healthy brain and tumor VOIs.

Condition2.reason: The segmentation was performed by a nuclear physician, so it is not fully automated.

Item8.reason: The study describes the segmentation methodology, including the use of LifeX software and a semi-automatic segmentation based on a threshold of 1.6 of mean standardized uptake value of healthy brain (SUVmean) to determine tumor VOIs.

Item9.reason: The segmentation is not fully automated, so this item is not applicable.

Item10.reason: The test set segmentation masks were not produced by a single reader or automated tool. The segmentation was performed by a nuclear physician (L.R.).

Condition3.reason: The study extracts radiomic features, which are hand-crafted features.

Item11.reason: The study describes the image pre-processing techniques used, including resampling to correct for different voxel sizes, correction for patient movements, and denoising using the HYPR-LR method.

Item12.reason: The study used the pyradiomics package for feature extraction, which is a standardized software.

Item13.reason: The study describes the feature types (statistical, histogram-based, local-intensity and textural features) and provides information on the parameters used for feature extraction.

Condition4.reason: The study includes tabular data in the form of extracted radiomic features.

Condition5.reason: The study does not use end-to-end deep learning.

Item14.reason: The study performs dimensionality reduction using hierarchical clustering based on an absolute spearman correlation coefficient (SCC) as distance matrix and a threshold of 0.9.

Item15.reason: The study performs dimensionality reduction using hierarchical clustering based on an absolute spearman correlation coefficient (SCC) as distance matrix and a threshold of 0.9.

Item16.reason: The study uses hierarchical clustering to reduce the number of features.

Item17.reason: The study does not use end-to-end deep learning, so this item is not applicable.

Item18.reason: The study splits the data into training and test sets from different hospital centers. All transformations and algorithms were fitted using only the training set and were subsequently applied to the test set.

Item19.reason: The study considered clinical features, including age, sex, histopathological WHO grade, IDH mutation status, 1p/19q codeletion status, previous tumor resection and contrast enhancement on MRI.

Item20.reason: The study reports appropriate accuracy metrics, such as AUC, accuracy, precision, F1 score, and balanced accuracy.

Item21.reason: The study reports 95% confidence intervals (CI) for the performance metrics.

Item22.reason: The study does not explicitly mention calibration assessment of the final model.

Item23.reason: The study does not perform uni-parametric imaging or provide proof of its inferiority. The study uses both static and dynamic PET data.

Item24.reason: The study compares the radiomics models to a reference model based on TBRmean, which is representative of the clinical practice.

Item25.reason: The study does not compare with a simple or classical statistical model. The reference model is based on TBRmean, but it is not a simple baseline reference model like a Zero Rules classifier.

Item26.reason: The model is tested on an independent data set that is sampled from the same source as the training and/or validation sets.

Item27.reason: The model is tested with independent data from another institution (external validation).

Item28.reason: The study provides the extracted data in a Supplementary Table S1.

Item29.reason: The study does not explicitly state that all scripts related to automatic segmentation and/or modeling are shared with the public.

Item30.reason: The study does not share the final model in the form of a raw model file or as a ready-to-use system.
